topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the weight of a dropped textbook. Professor Albright was departing. My stomach immediately tightened into a knot, a cold, hard thing that refused to loosen. I reread the subject line, "Departure Notice," three times. Every sentence of his last correspondence—the gentle corrections, the enthusiastic encouragement—felt suddenly brittle, like old glass about to shatter. I spent the entire afternoon staring at the blinking cursor on my screen, unable to formulate a reply. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, twitching slightly. When I finally typed, it was a clumsy, overly formal acknowledgement. The silence in my small apartment amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I felt a distinct urge to hide under my duvet, to disappear from the sudden, exposed vulnerability of this academic limbo.

[story 2]

"So, that’s it?" I managed to squeak out, the sound thin and reedy in the empty faculty lounge. I watched Dr. Evans pack his worn leather satchel, the way he carefully placed his favorite worn copy of Kant into it. My palms were slick against the cheap plastic of my water bottle. I kept catching my reflection in the darkened window—my face pale, my posture rigid, as if bracing for an impact that wasn't physically present. I forced a smile, a tight, unnatural stretching of the muscles around my mouth, and tried to sound casual when I spoke about the upcoming revisions. Internally, a frantic monologue was running: *What if I mess this up now? What if I can't measure up to the standard he set?* The air felt thick, suffocating, and I wanted nothing more than to melt into the linoleum floor.

[story 3]

The departmental meeting felt like a spotlight had been physically switched on. Everyone seemed to be exchanging knowing glances, their smiles too wide, their nods too eager. When the Dean announced Professor Albright’s transition, a strange numbness washed over me, followed by a sharp, hot flush creeping up my neck. I gripped the edge of the table so tightly my knuckles turned white. I focused intently on the pattern of the carpet beneath my shoes, counting the fibers, anything to avoid meeting anyone’s eyes. Every word spoken felt magnified, scrutinized. I felt a profound sense of inadequacy bloom in my chest, a hollow ache where confidence should have been. I mumbled a quick apology for being late, a small, insignificant noise swallowed instantly by the room’s sudden, expectant quiet.
